coredata.dc:title: Color-tunable carbon dots via control the degree of self-assembly in solution at different concentration
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Tong
Zhao, Yue
Matras-Postolek, Katarzyna
Yang, Ping
coredata.dc:description: Using carbon spheres and sucrose as the carbon sources, two kinds of color-tunable carbon dots (Cdots) named as Cdots-C and Cdots-S were prepared via change the solution concentration. As the concentration of Cdots solution increased, varying degrees of self-assembly occur between monodisperse Cdots. Hence aggregates with different types formed, which can be observed. Appearance of new excitation bands at 465 and 520 nm caused by H- and J-type Cdots aggregates caused the emission at long wavelengths. At the same time, the excitation band at 300–400 nm induced blue-green emission and the competitions of three excitation bands resulted in the color-tunable PL behaviour which cover the region from green to red emission. The difference of carbon sources of Cdots-C, Cdots-S and Cdots-G (graphite) explained the reason why concentration-dependent PL behaviour were not observed in Cdots-G at a high solution concentration. The process of self-assembly were ascribed to hydrogen bonding interaction between abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of Cdots-C and Cdots-S. This work provided a simple approach to modulate photoluminescence (PL) color of Cdots and will extend the application in bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Self-assembly
Photoluminescence
Hydrogen bonding

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) PL spectra of Cdots-C solutions with increasing concentrations excited at their optimal λ ex. (b) PL spectra of Cdots-C solutions with increasing concentrations excited at 420 nm. (c) Absorption spectra of Cdots-C solutions with increasing concentrations. (d) Relationship of corresponding concentrations of Cdots-C solutions and their absorption values at 500 nm. (e) Optical photographs of Cdots-C solutions. The concentrations of Cdots-C solutions increased from 0.10 mg/ml to 2.00 mg/ml.
(a) TEM (b) HRTEM (c) AFM image of Cdots-C at a low concentration. (d,e) TEM (f) AFM image of Cdots-C at a relative high concentration.
(a) FTIR spectrum of Cdots-C. (b) FTIR spectrum of Cdots-G.

(a) PL spectra of Cdots-S solutions with increasing concentrations excited at their optimal λ ex. (b) PL spectra of Cdots-S solutions with increasing concentrations excited at 420 nm. The concentrations of Cdots-S solutions increased from 0.10 mg/ml to 2.50 mg/ml.

(a) PL excitation spectra of Cdots-C and Cdots-S at different concentrations (from 0.10 to 2.00 mg/ml) when the λ em were fixed at 575 nm. (b) PL excitation spectra of Cdots-C and Cdots-S at different λ em (from 500 to 650 nm). The concentration of Cdots-C and Cdots-S aqueous solution is ∼0.25 mg/ml.
Illustration of formation process of Cdots assemblies.
Preparation conditions of Cdots-C, Cdots-S and Cdots-G.

head.ce:title: Color-tunable carbon dots via control the degree of self-assembly in solution at different concentration

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Engineering and Technology, Cracow University of Technology, Krakow, Poland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using carbon spheres and sucrose as the carbon sources, two kinds of color-tunable carbon dots (Cdots) named as Cdots-C and Cdots-S were prepared via change the solution concentration. As the concentration of Cdots solution increased, varying degrees of self-assembly occur between monodisperse Cdots. Hence aggregates with different types formed, which can be observed. Appearance of new excitation bands at 465 and 520 nm caused by H- and J-type Cdots aggregates caused the emission at long wavelengths. At the same time, the excitation band at 300–400 nm induced blue-green emission and the competitions of three excitation bands resulted in the color-tunable PL behaviour which cover the region from green to red emission. The difference of carbon sources of Cdots-C, Cdots-S and Cdots-G (graphite) explained the reason why concentration-dependent PL behaviour were not observed in Cdots-G at a high solution concentration. The process of self-assembly were ascribed to hydrogen bonding interaction between abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of Cdots-C and Cdots-S. This work provided a simple approach to modulate photoluminescence (PL) color of Cdots and will extend the application in bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, as a new member of carbon material family, carbon dots (Cdots) have attracted extensive attention due to their photobleaching resistance, low toxicity, good biocompatibility [1] and convenience of conducting doping [2] or surface modification [3]. Cdots are widely used in various fields such as light-emitting devices, vivo imaging, chemical sensing and photocatalysis [4]. As the research progressed further, the range of carbon source continued to expand, and more and more synthesis methods emerged. Among them, strong acidic oxidation method was first used by Liu et al. [5]. Cdots with different fluorescence were obtained via reflux treat candle ash in concentrated nitric acid for 12 h and a series of purification treatments such as centrifugation, dialysis and electrophoresis. This process is complicated and the as-prepared sample exhibit low photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs). After that, researchers continued to optimize it by various strategies [6,7], but the obtained Cdots still exhibited relative weak PL and their emission wavelengths were mostly limited to bule-green emission region. These problems and the ambiguous PL mechanism restricted the application of Cdots. Thus, it is desired to seek a method to control PL behaviour and explore the PL mechanism.

Compared with semiconductor quantum dots, Cdots have more surface groups with complicated composition because of preparation procedures. Hence the tunable PL behaviour was mainly attributed to the difference in size and surface state [8]. Recently, apart from heteroatom doping [9,10] and some other methods [11–13] to control emission wavelength, some reports also investigated the effect of solution conditions such as PH [14], concentration [15,16] and polarity [17] of solution. During these researches, self-assembly or aggregation of Cdots were proved to be related to PL behaviour of Cdots greatly and the PL mechanism were discussed. Sharma et al. investigated the self-assembly and molecular origin of Cdots in different solvents [18]. The aggregation-induced spectral splitting of the main excitation band and the temperature-dependent PL results gave the evidence that Cdots self-assembled into various H-aggregates. Aggregation induced orange-red emission which is independent of polarity of medium were observed by Gude et al. and they hold that aggregated hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) derivatives are responsible for PL of Cdots [19]. While in Cdots aqueous solution, the mechanism of self-assembly process and concentration-dependent tunable PL behaviour still lack of detailed explanation.

In this work, three kinds of Cdots were prepared through a reflux treatment in concentrated mixed-acid using cheap and easily available graphite powder (Cdots-G), carbon sphere (Cdots-C), and sucrose (Cdots-S) as the carbon sources. For Cdots-C and Cdots-S, the regular self-assembly of Cdots monomers in aqueous solutions was observed when the concentration increased. Meanwhile tunable PL behaviour of Cdots-C/S aqueous solutions with different concentration were observed. But this concentration-dependent PL phenomenon was not observed for Cdots-G. A possible mechanism was proposed to explain the process of self-assembly. Numerous hydroxyl groups on the surface of Cdots-C/S were proved to be responsible for the self-assembly of Cdots through hydrogen bond interaction. What's more, appearance of excitation bands in the visible wavelength indicated the formation of H and J-type Cdots aggregates [20]. The tunable PL behaviour was ascribed to the competition of three excitation bands. This facile strategy of preparing multi-color fluorescent Cdots will facilitate the application in bio-imaging.

Graphite powder was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Sucrose was supplied from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Carbon sphere were prepared according to the method in literature [21]. Concentrated sulfuric acid and concentrated nitric acid were taken from Tianjin Guangcheng Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Sodium hydroxide was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd.

The excitation and emission spectra of samples were recorded by a fluorometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan) with a xenon lamp as an excitation light source. The absorption spectra were measured at room temperature using an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (U-4100, Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared absorption spectroscopy (FTIR) was used by an infrared spectrometer (Nicolet 380, Thermo, America). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) photographs were taken with a high resolution transmission electron microscope (Tecani F20, FEI, America). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) photographs were obtained from Multimode 8 of Bruker, Germany. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured from an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Ultima IV, Japan. X-ray, Cu Kα (λ = 0.15406 nm)).

Synthesis of carbon spheres: 1.5 M aqueous solution of sucrose was prepared, which was transferred into a Teflon-clave and maintained at 190 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, the powder was calcined in argon atmosphere. At first, the temperature was raised to 500 °C with a rate of 1 °C/min and then increased to 1000 °C in a rate of 5 °C/min. After centrifuging and washing, the black powder (carbon spheres) were obtained.

Synthesis of Cdots-C: 100 mg of carbon sphere was added into 20 ml mixed acid (concentrated sulfuric acid: concentrated nitric acid = 3:1) and sonicated for 30 min, then transferred it to a three-necked flask and kept the temperature at 90 °C for 24 h. After reflux, the solution was neutralized to neutral with sodium hydroxide solution of 1 M. The large particles were removed by filtration through a 0.22 μm filter and excess acid and salt were removed by dialysis in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 48 h (replaced deionized water every 8 h). The obtained yellow transparent liquid was the colloidal solution of Cdots-C.

Synthesis of Cdots-S: 5 ml of deionized water was added to 10 g of sucrose and stirred vigorously until the sucrose was completely dissolved to form a clear solution. 20 ml of mixed acid (concentrated sulfuric acid: concentrated nitric acid = 3:1) was then added and sonicated for 30 min. Then sample was transferred to a three-necked flask and kept at 120 °C for 24 h. After reflux, the solution was neutralized to neutral with 1 M sodium hydroxide solution. Large particles were removed by filtration through a 0.22 μm filter and dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 48 h to remove excess acid, salt and sucrose (replaced deionized water every 8 h). The obtained orange transparent liquid was the colloidal solution of Cdots-S.

Synthesis of Cdots-G: 100 mg of graphite powder was added into 20 ml of mixed acid (concentrated sulfuric acid: concentrated nitric acid = 3:1) and sonicated for 30 min, then sample was transferred to a three-necked flask and kept the temperature at 90 °C for 24 h. After reflux, the solution was neutralized to neutral with a sodium hydroxide solution (1 M). Large particles were removed by filtration through a 0.22 μm filter and excess acid and salt were removed by dialysis in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 48 h (replaced deionized water every 8 h). Finally, transparent yellow Cdots-G colloidal solution was obtained. The preparation conditions of Cdots-C, Cdots-S and Cdots-G samples were listed in Table 1 .

According to the preparation method described in literature [21], porous carbon spheres were obtained as a carbon source of Cdots-C. The carbon spheres exhibit standard spherical shape and nanopores were distributed on the surface, which facilitate the oxidation and crushing process of the mixed-acid. After dialysis treatment, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain solid and a series of Cdots-C aqueous solutions with different concentrations (from 0.10 to 2.00 mg/ml) were prepared. To investigate the optical properties of Cdots-C, we characterized these samples using fluorometer. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), when the concentration increases, obvious red shift of emission wavelengths (from 538 to 655 nm) were observed when excited by their maximum excitation wavelengths, which cover the region from green to red eventually. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were listed in Table S1 (see Supporting Information) and optical photographs of samples were shown in Fig. 1(e). The excitation-dependent property of Cdots-C were explored as shown in Fig. S1 (see Supporting Information). So to rule out the effect of the excitation-dependent nature, PL spectra of Cdots-C samples were recorded excited at 420 nm as shown in Fig. 1(b). Similar phenomenon emerged and their emission wavelengths were listed in Table S2 (see Supporting Information), indicating that the red shift of λ em is due to the change of Cdots-C solution state rather than excitation light wavelengths. What's more, the absorption spectra of Cdots-C aqueous solution at different concentrations were recorded as revealed in Fig. 1(c) and the values at 500 nm were selected to characterize the concentration of Cdots-C aqueous solution. The relationship of corresponding concentrations and their absorption values at 500 nm were fitted into a line as shown in Fig. 1(d) and the function is Y = 0.493X-0.033.

In order to confirm the state of Cdots-C in solution at low and high concentration, samples were characterized by TEM, HRTEM and AFM. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), at low concentration, the obtained Cdots-C are spherical and uniform with diameters of 2–3 nm. HRTEM image in Fig. 2(b) exhibited clearly lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, which is close to the (100) plane of graphene [22], suggesting Cdots-C with ideal crystallinity. AFM images in Fig. 2(c) revealed that the heights of Cdots-C are around 1.2 nm, suggesting that it is consist of 1–3 graphene layers [23]. These results confirmed that Cdots-C is monodisperse at a low concentration. Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Cdots-C (Fig. S2, see Supporting Information) revealed a broad peak at ∼25°, which also confirm the sample with graphene structure [24]. Then when we increased the sample concentration, as shown in Fig. 2(d), large Cdots-C assemblies with an average size of ∼12 nm were observed. Seemingly, this is not like simple agglomeration of monodisperse Cdots-C in the solution. Namely, several single Cdots-C assembled in solution to form a nanocluster [25]. The assembly was ascribed to the electrostatic attracting from hydrogen bonding between monodisperse Cdots-C and we will discuss the detailed process in following section. When we continue to increase the concentration of sample, these nanoclusters became larger (∼20 nm) as shown in Fig. 2(e). Furthermore, AFM image of Cdots-C at a relative high concentration were revealed in Fig. 2(f). Compared with the monodisperse Cdots-C in Fig. 2(c), the average size of Cdots-C assemblies further increased to ∼40 nm and height increased to about 7 nm. These characterizations above further indicated that the degree of self-assembly between monodisperse Cdots-C depended on their amount in the solution, thereby nanoclusters with different sizes formed.

In order to determine the formation mechanism of Cdots-C assemblies in the solution at high concentration, FTIR spectrum were used to confirm the functional groups on the surface of Cdots-C. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), a broad band at 3430 cm−1 and a peak at 3140 cm−1 came from the stretching modes of OH and NH, respectively [26]. The peak at 1640 cm−1 related to the stretching vibration of CO and the CO stretching vibrations was measured at 1060 cm−1, indicating there are plenty of O-containing groups [27]. CN stretching vibration bands were found at 1180 and 1400 cm−1 [28]. It is noteworthy that a strong OH absorption peak emerged. For further determine the source of OH, graphite powder was used to synthesize the comparative sample (Cdots-G). The synthesis method is identical to that of Cdots-C. As shown in Fig. S3 (a) (see Supporting Information), Cdots-G had a uniform size of 2–4 nm and the lattice parameter was 0.23 nm too. FTIR spectrum of Cdots-G was revealed in Fig. 3(b). Compare the OH vibrations intensity of the two samples, there may be a large amount of OH on the surface of Cdots-C in addition to the influence of water molecules absorbed during the test process. And as shown in Fig. S3 (b) (see Supporting Information), at a relative high concentration, the self-assembly or agglomeration did not occur because of few OH groups on the surface of Cdots-G. Therefore, we assumed that OH groups play an important role during the formation of Cdots-C assemblies. On account of the synthesis methods are identical, precursors used in experimental mainly affect the concentration-dependent PL behaviour. Considering that the carbon source of porous carbon spheres is sucrose, which as a kind of polyhydroxy aldehyde formed by the condensation and dehydration process of the hemiacetal hydroxyl groups on glucose and fructose. The abundant OH on the surface of Cdots-C were speculated to come from sucrose. Hence Cdots-S was prepared by carbonizing sucrose in concentrated mixed-acid directly. As far as the experiment method is concerned, the temperature increased to 120 °C to accelerate the carbonization process. During reflux, the color of the solution was changed from transparent colorless to orange-red, at the same time, a large amount of gas let out. These phenomenon similar to the hydrothermal reaction of glucose indicated the formation of some aromatic compounds and the carbonization process of sucrose occurred [29].

After dialysis treatment, as the same process of Cdots-C, the sample was freeze-dried to obtain solid and a series of Cdots-S aqueous solutions with different concentrations (from 0.10 to 2.50 mg/ml) were prepared too. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the PL spectra of these samples resembled that of Cdots-C. Table S3 illustrated the optimal λ ex and λ em wavelengths of Cdots-S with different concentrations. The PL peak wavelengths of Cdots-S shifted from 675 to 500 nm as the concentration decreased. This phenomenon was more pronounced than that of Cdots-C and the reason can be attribute to the obvious excitation-dependent behaviour of Cdots-S as shown in Fig. S4 (see Supporting Information). PL spectra excited at a fixed excitation wavelength (420 nm) were recorded as revealed in Fig. 4(b) and their emission wavelengths were listed in Table S4. Absorption spectra of Cdots-S aqueous solution at different concentrations were recorded as revealed in Fig. S5 (a) (see Supporting Information) and the relationship of corresponding concentrations and their absorption values at 500 nm were fitted into a line similar to that of Cdots-C as shown in Fig. S5 (b) (see Supporting Information) and the function is Y = 0.729X-0.013. At the same time, TEM and AFM images exhibited the different states of Cdots-S aqueous solution at a low concentration (Fig. 4(c)) and high concentrations (Fig. 4(d and e)). As revealed in Fig. 4 (c), Cdots-S present spherical morphology with a diameter of ∼3 nm and the clear lattice spacing of 0.23 nm observed in the inset indicated its ideal crystallinity. Then with the concentration increased, these monodisperse Cdots-S self-assembled into bigger nanocluster with a diameter of 40–50 nm as shown in Fig. 4 (d). This result is further confirmed by AFM image as shown in Fig. 4(e), in which the sizes and heights of nanoclusters increased to about 30 and 12 nm respectively at a relative high concentration. According to the FTIR spectrum in Fig. S6 (see Supporting Information), apart from similar functional groups as Cdots-C and Cdots-G, considerable OH also presented on the surface of Cdots-S.

Hence a possible formation process of Cdots assemblies through hydrogen bonding interaction to obtain nanoclusters at a high solution concentration was illustrated in Scheme 1 . As we all know, H atom in the hydroxyl group connect with O atom which possess great electronegativity by covalent bond. When concentration of Cdots solution increased, the relative distance between OH groups on the surface of two monodispersed Cdots reduced significantly. When the H atom was close to O atom on the other hydroxyl group, a kind of interaction in form of OH⋯O formed between the two O atoms using H atom as a medium. This special interaction is hydrogen bonding. Specifically, H atom on the hydroxyl group has only one electron for forming covalent bond with O atom. While the electronegativity of O atom is great and electrons is easy to be attracted, so that the electron pair is close to the O atom. Therefore, H atom is in an electron-deficient state, so O and H atom exhibit negative and positive charge respectively. The positive and negative charges attract each other to form a hydrogen bond. So by varying the concentration of Cdots solution, the amount of Cdots in the solution was modulated and then the distance between monodisperse Cdots could also be controlled. Thus self-assembly of Cdots occurred and formed nanoclusters. These nanoclusters were proved to be Cdots aggregates with different types [20] by its excitation spectra and their effects on tunable PL behaviour were investigated as follows.

To further make clear the concentration-dependent PL behaviour origin of Cdots, excitation spectra were recorded at different concentrations of solution as shown in Fig. 5 (a). The emission wavelength was fixed at 575 nm. Multiple excitation bands were observed at around 520, 465 and the region between 300 and 400 nm, respectively. Among them, the excitation band at 300–400 nm was attribute to Cdots monomers [20]. In the visible wavelengths region, the two peaks at 465 and 520 nm were proved to be related to H and J-type Cdots aggregates respectively. It is the reason why the intensity of the two excitation bands at 465 and 520 nm elevated with the concentration gradually increased. To further explore the effect of the excitation bands at 300–400 nm, absorption spectra in a dilute Cdots solution were recorded as revealed in Fig. S7 (see Supporting Information). The absorption peak at ∼250 nm in the UV region was assigned to π-π* transition from the core of Cdots [30] and this transition did not induce any emission, which was not observed in excitation spectrum. The n-π* transition [15] resulted in the broad absorption band at 300–400 nm came from the surface of Cdots, which brought about the blue-green emission of monodisperse Cdots. Because the emission from surface energy transition is easier to be affect by the concentration compared with core energy transition [15], Cdots aggregates exhibited emission at long wavelength due to the surface energy transfer [16,31]. Hence the tunable PL behaviour can be explained as follows. Specially, in a dilute Cdots solution, PL behaviour was mainly controlled by monodisperse Cdots and blue-green emission was observed excitated at 300–400 nm. Then with the solution concentration increased, monodisperse Cdots self-assembled into aggregates with different types via hydrogen bonding interaction between abundant OH groups on the surface of Cdots. Then the effect of Cdots monomers to PL behaviour decreased and the excitation band at 465 and 520 nm begin to play crucial roles. Because the surface energy is easier to transfer between Cdots aggregates, the two excitation bands induced long-wavelength emission, which resulted in yellow and red emission. Ultimately the competition of the three excitation bands resulted in the color-tunable PL behaviour controlled by Cdots aqueous solution concentration. Furthermore, PL excitation spectra at different emission wavelengths (500, 550, 600, 650 nm) further indicated the three excitation bands as revealed in Fig. 5 (b). Intense excitation bands at 465 and 520 nm were all observed in Cdots-C and Cdots-S aqueous solution, which further indicated that the emission at long wavelength originate from Cdots self-assemblies. The excitation band at 300–400 nm in Cdots-S exhibited a red shift due to the relative high solution concentration. And this band were almost covered as a result of the intense excitation bands of aggregates in Cdots-C aqueous solution.

In conclusion, three types of Cdots were prepared by treating different carbon sources with concentrated mixed-acid. Compared with Cdots-G made from graphite powder, Cdots-C and Cdots-S which synthesized by carbon spheres and sucrose both exhibited special concentration-dependent PL phenomenon. Self-assembly of Cdots with different degrees were observed in Cdots-C/S aqueous solution at high concentration and the process was investigated. Hydrogen bond interaction was ascribed to be responsible for the self-assembly process due to the abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of Cdots-C/S. Furthermore, appearance of excitation bands at 465 and 520 nm indicated the formation of H and J-type Cdots aggregates, where the surface energy transfer took place and induced long-wavelength emission. The two excitation bands accompany with the excitation band at 300–400 nm modulated the PL behaviour of Cdots. This method provided a simple approach to prepare multicolor emission Cdots and will facilitate its application in imaging region.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the projects from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51572109 and 51772130).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2019.04.025.
